claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child or children you're claiming for:**

1. **How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?**
2. **What are their ages?** (This is important as eligibility rules differ based on whether the child is under 16, between 16-19, or 20 and over)

Please share the name(s) and age(s) of each child, and I'll guide you through the eligibility requirements for each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I'm looking to claim for my two children - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to meet you, and I'm happy to help you figure out your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14).

Since both of your children are under 16 years old, let me check the next requirement in the eligibility process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, for children under 16, I need to verify:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their young ages (4 and 14), I expect they're not working full-time, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 4, so obviously not working, and Blake is still in school - they don't have any job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in the child's own name (not as part of your family claim).

Given their ages, this is unlikely, but I need to confirm - are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - Alex is only 4 and Blake is 14, so they don't claim anything themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's perfectly clear. Let's continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in an apprenticeship?**

This includes:
- Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, or Degree Apprenticeships in England
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland  
- Apprenticeships in Wales (except Foundation level)

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14, I expect neither is in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in any apprenticeship. Alex is only 4, and Blake is 14 and still in school - too young for an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since neither child is in an apprenticeship, let's move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through ANY of these routes:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources (not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to you?** Most people will simply answer that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm based in Manchester. I'm not in any of those special situations with settled status or anything like that, I just live here normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You can meet this requirement through ANY of these ways:

1. **You live with the children** (most common)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the Child Benefit amount for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you support them in another way?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives with their other parent. However, I do help out financially with Alex - I contribute towards their clothes, food, school things, that sort of stuff.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information as the children are in different living situations.

**For Alex (who lives with the other parent):** You mentioned you contribute towards clothes, food, and school things. To meet the responsibility requirement, you need to contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay.

Let me check the current Child Benefit rates for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the exact current payment rates aren't listed in my specification, but as a general guide, Child Benefit is currently around £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children (though you should verify the exact current rates on gov.uk).

**For Alex:** Are you contributing at least the equivalent of what Child Benefit would pay (approximately £100+ per month) towards Alex's care? This can include:
- Money for food, clothes, school supplies
- Housing costs
- Pocket money
- Presents
- Any regular or lump sum contributions

**For Blake:** You live with Blake, so you automatically meet the responsibility requirement for them.

Can you confirm that your financial contributions for Alex meet or exceed the Child Benefit amount?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute about £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep - so that's around £200 a month. I help with clothes, school dinners, activities, those sorts of things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since you're contributing £50 per week (about £200 per month) towards Alex's upkeep, that definitely meets the requirement of contributing at least the Child Benefit amount. 

So to summarize:
- **Blake:** You live with Blake, so you meet the responsibility requirement ✓
- **Alex:** You contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep, meeting the requirement ✓

Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now this is an important question, especially since Alex lives with their other parent:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

This is particularly relevant for Alex since they live with the other parent. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

- Is Alex's other parent currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?
- Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

If someone else is claiming, have they agreed to transfer the claim to you, or would this be disputed?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex's other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. And nobody else is claiming for Blake either - it's just me looking to claim for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about the children's living situations. 

**For both Alex and Blake, are either of them:**
- Currently in hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally (at home with you or with Alex's other parent)?

What's the current situation for each child?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both just living normally - Blake is at home with me, and Alex is living with their other parent. Neither of them are in hospital, care, or being fostered or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since both children are living normally, let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council providing financial support for either child's housing or upkeep? Given that Blake lives with you and Alex lives with their other parent, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards either of them. Blake lives with me and I support them, and Alex lives with their other parent. The council isn't involved at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Since the council is not paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance, we've reached the final step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible!

**You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake!**

### Summary of Your Eligibility:

✅ **Alex (age 4):**
- Qualifies as a child under 16
- You meet the responsibility requirement through your financial contributions (£50/week towards their upkeep)
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- All other requirements are met

✅ **Blake (age 14):**
- Qualifies as a child under 16  
- You meet the responsibility requirement by living with them
- No one else is claiming for Blake
- All other requirements are met

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply for Child Benefit:**
   - Apply online at gov.uk, or
   - Fill out form CH2 and send it by post

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Birth certificates or adoption certificates for both Alex and Blake
   - Your child's identity documents

3. **Payment information:**
   - Payments are typically made every 4 weeks
   - If you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits, you may get weekly payments

4. **Important note about High Income:**
   - If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge applies
   - This doesn't affect your eligibility, but you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through your tax return
   - You can still claim even if this applies to you, as it protects your National Insurance credits

**Find out more and apply at:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit claim?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (clothes, food, school expenses), which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and meets the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally with their other parent, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives with Blake, which automatically meets the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester, UK). Both children are under 16 years old and meet all the qualifying child criteria. The claimant is responsible for both children - living with Blake and contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and neither child is in hospital, care, or being fostered. The local council is not paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility requirements are satisfied for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (clothes, food, school expenses), which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and meets the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally with their other parent, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives with Blake, which automatically meets the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and the council is not paying towards their accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester, UK). Both children are under 16 years old and meet all the qualifying child criteria. The claimant is responsible for both children - living with Blake and contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and neither child is in hospital, care, or being fostered. The local council is not paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility requirements are satisfied for both children.
